coredata.dc:title: Exploring the optimal ratio of d-glucose/l-aspartic acid for targeting carbon dots toward brain tumor cells
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Lihong
Sun, Tingting
Zheng, Xiaohua
Zheng, Min
Xie, Zhigang
coredata.dc:description: Targeting imaging to the desired site of action can increase the accuracy and effectiveness of diagnostic and treatment. In this work, a series of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by varying molar ratios of d-glucose (Glu) to l-aspartic acid (Asp). Their photophysical properties, morphologies and structures were investigated in detail. More important, the targeting ability was screened by confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry. The results indicate that CDs prepared from the optimal molar ratio of Glu/Asp (7:3) exhibit the highest targeting ability on C6 glioma cells. This work highlights the interplay of molecular design and corresponding functions, and open new possibility of developing state-of-art nanoparticles for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optimal ratio
d-Glucose
l-Aspartic acid
Carbon dots
Targeting brain cancer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CD73. The inset image is the diameter distribution of CD73. (B) High-resolution TEM image of CD73. (C) Highlighted areas of B. (D) AFM image of CD73. (E) UV–vis absorption and (F) PL spectra of CD73 under different excitation wavelengths.
Full XPS (A), High-resolution C1s (B) and N1s (C) XPS spectra of CD73. The XRD (D), Raman (E) and FT-IR (F) spectra of CD73.

CLSM images of C6 and L929 cells treated with CD19 (A, a), CD28 (B, b), CD37 (C, c), CD46 (D, d), CD55 (E, e), CD64 (F, f), CD73 (G, g), CD82 (H, h), CD91 (I, i) under 488nm excitation. (J) Mean fluorescence intensities of (A to I) and (a to i) determined by Image-Pro Plus 6.0 software. (K) The ratio of mean fluorescence intensities of C6 to L929 minus one for the nine samples.

Flow cytometric profiles of C6 and L929 cells incubated with CD19 (A, a), CD28 (B, b), CD37 (C, c), CD46 (D, d), CD55 (E, e), CD64 (F, f), CD73 (G, g), CD82 (H, h), CD91 (I, i) for 1h at 37°C. (J) Endocytosis rates in C6 and L929 cells. (K) The ratio of endocytosis rates of C6 to L929 minus one for nine samples. (L) In vitro biocompatibility of CD73 against C6 and L929 cell at 48h.
Schematic illustration of exploring the optimal ratio of Glu/Asp to prepare CDs for targeted brain tumor cells imaging.

head.ce:title: Exploring the optimal ratio of d-glucose/l-aspartic acid for targeting carbon dots toward brain tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Targeting imaging to the desired site of action can increase the accuracy and effectiveness of diagnostic and treatment. In this work, a series of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by varying molar ratios of d-glucose (Glu) to l-aspartic acid (Asp). Their photophysical properties, morphologies and structures were investigated in detail. More important, the targeting ability was screened by confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry. The results indicate that CDs prepared from the optimal molar ratio of Glu/Asp (7:3) exhibit the highest targeting ability on C6 glioma cells. This work highlights the interplay of molecular design and corresponding functions, and open new possibility of developing state-of-art nanoparticles for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Central nervous system (CNS) disorders could give rise to much significant hazardous for human health, for example, brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease and so on. Among them, glioma has been treated as one of the most deadly killers which is malignant primary brain tumors, because it is not only very difficult to find but also untoward to completely eradicate by surgery [1–5]. The main barriers involved the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which can prevent most substances from the blood into the brain tissue [6]. Thus, early diagnosis becomes the urgent demand [7]. Recently, fluorescence imaging technology has attracted increasing interest due to unique advantages in terms of good contrast, high sensitivity and easy operability [8–27].

As a new type of fluoresce imaging agents, carbon dots (CDs) open up a new avenue for fluorescence imaging because of the significant physical and chemical properties, such as tunable color, high stability, water solubility, pronounced biocompatibility as well as low toxicity [28–45]. Especially, some CDs were reported to have self-targeting abilities toward cancer cells, which is a great advantage over other fluorescent agents. The fluorescent materials usually need extra conjugation with targeting molecules for improving the selectivity and imaging contrast in the tumor site [46–52].

In the present work, a series of CDs (named CD19, CD28, CD37, CD46, CD55, CD64, CD73, CD82 and CD91) were obtained by varying the molar ratios of d-glucose (Glu)/l-aspartic acid (Asp) (1,9, 2,8, 3:7, 4:6, 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2 and 9:1). Laser confocal scanning microscopy (LCSM) imaging and flow cytometry analysis were conducted to compare the selectivity of the CDs on C6 glioma cells. The results indicate that CD73 has the highest targeting ability on C6 cells and can act as a perfect imaging and targeting agent for noninvasive glioma diagnosis (Scheme 1 ).

d-Glucose and l-aspartic acid (Asp) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai), other reagents were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Beijing). All the reagents were used without further treatment.

High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were recorded with a FEI-TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique Surface Analysis. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer. The crystalline structure was recorded by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker AXS D8 Focus), using Cu Ka radiation (λ=1.54056Å). Raman spectra were conducted on Horiba JY LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer LS-55 phosphorescence spectrophotometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were conducted on Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Zeta potential of the nanoparticles was analyzed using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd.). Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were captured on the Multimode 8 (Bruker Co.) in tapping mode. CLSM images were conducted on Zeiss LSM 780 imaging system. Flow cytometry assay was carried out on Becton Dickinson FACSAria sorting flow cytometer (Becton-Dickinson, Mountain View, US).

The CDs were prepared through a thermal pyrolysis method with Glu and Asp as raw materials at different molar ratios (1:9, 2:8, 3:7, 4:6, 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2 and 9:1). The mixtures of Glu and Asp were loaded into nine breakers respectively and 1M NaOH were added. The reaction mixtures were placed inside the oven and heated to 125°C and kept for 30min. Then heated to 200°C and maintained for 20min. After that, the crude products were cooled to room temperature naturally. The products were dissolved into 20mL of water and subjected to dialysis. Solid CDs were obtained via freeze-drying method.

L929 mouse fibroblast cells (L929 cell) and C6 Brain Glioma cells (C6 cell) were purchased from the Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, China. The cells were grown in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, GIBCO) supplemented with 10% heat inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, GIBCO), 100U/mL penicillin and 100μg/mL streptomycin (Sigma), and the culture medium was replaced once every day.

L929 and C6 cell lines harvested in a logarithmic growth phase were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 105 cells per well and incubated in DMEM for 24h, respectively. The medium was then replaced by CDs at a concentration from 10 to 750μg/mL. The incubation was continued for 48h. Then, 20μL of MTT solution in PBS with the concentration of 5mgmL−1 was added and the plates were incubated for another 4h at 37°C. Followed by removal of the culture medium containing MTT and addition of 150μL of DMSO to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals formed. Finally, the plates were shaken for 10min, and the absorbance of formazan product was measured at 490nm by a microplate reader.

Cellular uptakes by L929 and C6 cells were examined using a CLSM. L929 cells and C6 cells were seeded in 6-well culture plates (a sterile cover slip was put in each well) at a density of 5×104 cells per well and allowed to adhere for 24h. After that, the cells were treated with CDs (2mgmL−1) for 1h at 37°C. And then the supernatant was carefully removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the cells were fixed with 800μL of 4% formaldehyde in each well for 20min at room temperature and washed twice with PBS again. The slides were mounted and observed with a CLSM imaging system.

The data were expressed as mean±standard deviation (SD). Student's t-test was used to determine the statistical difference between various experimental and control groups. Differences were considered statistically significant at a level of p <0.05.

Flow Cytometry Scanning (FCS) Cellular uptake by C6 and L929 cells was examined with FCS. Each cell line was seeded in six-well culture plates at a density of 5×104 cells per well and allowed to adhere for 24h. Hereafter the cells were treated with CD-Asp for 1h at 37°C. Soon afterwards, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS three times and treated with trypsin. Then, 1.0mL of PBS was added to each culture well, and the solutions were centrifuged for 5min at 5000rpm. After the removal of the supernatants, the cells were suspended in 0.5mL of PBS. Data for the 10,000 gated events were collected, and analyses were performed by flow cytometry (Beckman, California).

CD19, CD28, CD37, CD46, CD55, CD64, CD73, CD82 and CD91 were synthesized through a thermal pyrolysis method with Glu and Asp as raw materials at different molar ratios of 1:9, 2:8, 3:7, 4:6, 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2 and 9:1, respectively. The reactions parameters and yields of products were listed in Table S1. Taking CD73 as an example, 1M of NaOH solution were added to the aqueous of Glu (1.26g, 7mmol) and Asp (0.339g, 3mmol) to adjust pH to 8.0. The reaction mixture was heated to 125°C and kept for 30min, then continue to be heated up to 200°C and maintain at 200°C for 20min. The crude product was cooled to room temperature naturally, and dissolved into water and subjected to dialysis. The purified product was freeze-dried to form the brown solid with yield of 37.3%.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were conducted to characterize the morphologies and sizes. As shown in Fig. 1A, CD73 is monodispersed in water with an average diameter of 2.49±0.42nm, HR-TEM indicates that CD73 possess well-resolved lattice spacing value of 0.21nm (Fig. 1B and C), which corresponds to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Fig. 1D) show that CD73 is dispersing in water without obvious aggregation. The height of CD73 is 0.43 to 1.12nm based on the height profile along the line cut, revealing that there are 1–3 layers of graphene sheets.

The optical properties of nine samples were collected by UV–Vis absorption (Fig. S1) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. S2). Since all of the CDs have similar optical properties, take CD73 for example, it has a broad absorption band centered at 292nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1E), which is ascribed to π−π* transition of nanocarbon. The PL spectra of CD73 were illustrated in Fig. 1F, as excitation wavelength increases from 340 to 460nm, the emission gradually shifted from 493nm to 531nm with the strongest emission at 524nm when excited at 440nm. As we all know, with the excitation wavelength/emission wavelength increased, the depth of the probe tissue will be enhanced [34,53,54]. Full-spectrum PL behaviors of CDs provide a favorable condition for bio-imaging [55,56].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was aimed to explore the composition of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, there have four peaks at 284.67, 399.65, 496.0 and 532.09eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, NaKLL and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2B) can be well deconvolved into three peaks, which correspond to CC/CC (284.6eV), CN/CO (285.9eV) and CO2H (287.7eV), respectively. The high-resolution XPS band of N 1s can be fitted into two peaks at 399.4 and 400.9eV, related to pyridinic and pyrrolic N, respectively. All the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S3), and the results illustrate that all of them have crystalline nature. As for CD73 (Fig. 2D), there is a characteristic peak located at 2θ=26.1° (3.4Å) in its XRD pattern, what is similar with the graphite lattice spacing of (002). Nine samples were also analyzed on Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S4). Raman spectrum (Fig. 2E) of CD73 shows that it has two peaks at 1340cm−1 and 1557cm−1, which correspond to disordered D band (sp3-hybridized carbon) and crystalline G band (sp2-hybridized carbon), respectively, indicating that it has a similar structure with graphite. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were used to determine the functional groups on CDs (Fig. S5), Fig. 2F exhibits that OH, NH, CO, CO and COOH groups, corresponding to 3425cm−1, 3200cm−1, 1560cm−1, 1258cm−1 and 1759cm−1 are detected on the surface of CD73, which improve the hydrophilicity of CD73 in aqueous solution. The zeta potentials of CDs are listed in Table S2, all of them are negative, further confirms the presence of many hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on CDs.

Subsequently, CLSM (Fig. 3 ) was employed to compare the selectivity of the nine samples toward C6 cells. L929 and C6 cells were treated with nine samples (2mgmL−1) for 1h at 37°C, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3A–I and a–i, the cells displayed green color under the excitation of 488nm. Mean fluorescence intensities of (A to I) and (a to i) were analyzed by Image-Pro Plus 6.0 software, as exhibited by Fig. 3J, K and Table S3, little differences between C6 and L929 cells were observed for CD19, CD28, CD37, CD46 and CD64. As for CD55, CD73, CD82 and CD91, C6 cells were brighter than L929 cells in the sequence of CD82 <CD91 <CD55 <CD73, indicating that CD73 has the best targeting ability for C6 cells.

Flow cytometry scanning was used to quantify the endocytosis rates of C6 and L929 cells for the nine samples. As shown in Fig. 4 and Table S3, all the samples could be internalized into C6 and L929 cells. However, the uptake abilities of C6 and L929 cells are different. The biggest difference between C6 and L929 cells are observed for CD73 (Fig. 4K), and the endocytosis rate of C6 to L929 is 1.84, which is higher than the other eight samples, demonstrating that CD73 possess the highest selectivity toward C6 cell. This conclusion is consistent with results of LCSM.

The cytotoxicity of CD73 was determined via MTT method with C6 and L929 as test cell lines. As shown in Fig. 4L, the survival rates of the cells are over 80% even at the high dose, indicating the low cytotoxicity of CD73. The low cytotoxicity benefits to the imaging in living cells.

In summary, the CDs were prepared through a thermal pyrolysis method with d-glucose and l-aspartic acid as starting materials by varying molar ratios (1:9, 2:8, 3:7, 4:6, 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2 and 9:1). The selectivity of the nine samples on C6 cells were revealed by CLSM and flow cytometry analysis. The results indicate that CD73 has the highest selectivity toward C6 cells and can be used as a targeted imaging agent for noninvasive glioma diagnosis. This works provides a new imaging agent for selectively recognizing brain glioma, which could be used potentially in diagnosis and theranostic in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Science and Technology Research Project of Jilin Province (No. 20160101292JC) and the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province during the 13th Five-year Plan Period (No. 2016322).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.12.011.
